articles.title: Sagittal femoral morphology is not associated with robotic planning or soft-tissue balance during robotic-assisted total knee arthroplasty: An analysis from the Brescia Knee Research Group.
articles.authors: Andriollo L, Lazzaro R, Alborghetti M, Lucchetta L, Coppola D, Grassa D, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of prospectively maintained database
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary robotic-assisted total knee replacement
articles.outcomes: Relationship between sagittal femoral shape and robotic planning parameters; relationship between sagittal femoral shape and soft-tissue balance (medial-lateral laxity in extension and flexion)
articles.take_home: The shape of the thighbone in the front-to-back direction does not significantly affect how the surgeon plans the robotic knee replacement or how balanced the soft tissues are after surgery. This suggests that surgeons need to consider many different aspects of a patient's anatomy when planning knee replacement rather than relying on one measurement alone.

articles.title: Is the Incidence of Conversion from Unicompartmental to Total Knee Arthroplasty Higher Among Surgeons Who Do Not Have Arthroplasty Fellowship Training?
articles.authors: McCormick KL, Schaffer O, Novikov D, Rozell JC
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary unicompartmental knee arthroplasty at a single academic institution
articles.sample_size: 413 unicompartmental knee arthroplasties (202 by fellowship-trained surgeons, 211 by non-fellowship-trained surgeons)
articles.outcomes: Two-year and five-year conversion rates to total knee arthroplasty, implant survivorship, postoperative complications
articles.take_home: Surgeons with arthroplasty fellowship training had lower conversion rates to total knee replacement at two years (2.5% versus 5.7%), even though they treated older patients with more medical problems and used robotic assistance less often. Beyond two years, failure patterns were similar between the two groups.

articles.title: Rising Volume of Periprosthetic Fractures After Primary Total Hip and Knee Arthroplasty: A Contemporary Epidemiologic Analysis.
articles.authors: Heo KY, McCall M, Sheth N, Lee JD, Naeini ZV, Parry JA, et al.
articles.population: Patients presenting to emergency departments with periprosthetic hip and knee fractures
articles.outcomes: Annual emergency department visits, surgical procedures (fixation, arthroplasty, or combination), and hospital costs; projections to 2035
articles.take_home: Fractures around hip and knee replacements are becoming increasingly common, with emergency department visits expected to roughly double by 2035. Hospital costs per case are also projected to rise significantly, highlighting the need for better prevention strategies.

articles.title: Reassessing Total Joint Arthroplasty Case Volumes in The United States: Accounting for Ultra-Low-Volume Surgeons.
articles.authors: Culler MW, Iyer A, Lim MA, Schwarzkopf R, Lieberman JR, Heckmann ND
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of national insurance database
articles.population: Patients who underwent primary total knee arthroplasty or total hip arthroplasty in the United States
articles.sample_size: All patients in a national insurer database from 2016 to 2023
articles.outcomes: Surgeon case volume trends, percentage of ultra-low-volume surgeons, average annual caseload of surgeons treating typical TJA patients
articles.take_home: Most joint replacement patients are treated by high-volume surgeons, not low-volume ones. When ultra-low-volume surgeons are excluded from calculations, average surgeon caseload increased substantially from 2016 to 2023, showing that the surgeons who treat most patients are doing more procedures.

articles.title: Quarterly Trends in Total Knee Arthroplasty Utilization in the United States: Insurance Cost Sharing Drives Seasonal Variation.
articles.authors: Playter KP, Gonzalez MR, Elkadi S, Ly SM, Talmo CT, Smith EL
articles.population: Patients with a diagnosis of knee osteoarthritis in the United States
articles.outcomes: Quarterly trends in total knee arthroplasty utilisation across insurance cost-sharing groups; relationship between out-of-pocket costs and surgical volume
articles.take_home: Patients with higher out-of-pocket costs for knee replacement surgery tend to delay having the operation until the end of the year, possibly when their insurance deductibles have been met, whereas those with lower costs are more likely to have surgery earlier in the year. This pattern could help hospitals plan their schedules more efficiently.

articles.title: Cell-based therapy injections for the management of knee osteoarthritis: The ESSKA-ICRS consensus. Recommendations using the RAND/UCLA appropriateness method for different clinical scenarios.
articles.authors: de Girolamo L, Kon E, Laver L, Andriolo L, Boffa A, Bagge J, et al.
articles.study_type: Expert consensus using RAND/UCLA Appropriateness Method
articles.population: Patients with knee osteoarthritis across different ages, BMI categories and osteoarthritis severity levels
articles.sample_size: 144 clinical scenarios evaluated by ESSKA and ICRS expert panel
articles.outcomes: Appropriateness ratings (appropriate, inappropriate, or uncertain) for cell-based therapy injections across different patient scenarios
articles.take_home: Cell-based therapy injections for knee osteoarthritis work best in patients aged 65 years or under with healthy weight and moderate osteoarthritis, and should generally be avoided in patients over 80 years or with severe osteoarthritis. The type of cells used (from bone marrow or fat tissue) also affects suitability for different age groups.

articles.title: Coronal alignment behaviour across the knee range of motion is associated with constitutional alignment and soft-tissue balance: The REAL HKA classification.
articles.authors: Mylonakis N, Kenanidis E, Maslaris A, Sculco PK, Tsiridis E
articles.population: Patients undergoing robotic-assisted total knee arthroplasty with functional alignment
articles.outcomes: Intraoperative coronal alignment stability across knee range of motion at 0, 45 and 90 degrees of flexion; relationship between alignment phenotypes and CPAK and REAL classifications
articles.take_home: How a knee's alignment changes through bending varies depending on the patient's natural knee shape and ligament balance. About three-quarters of knees showed consistent alignment behaviour during surgery, but valgus knees were less stable.

articles.title: Femoral component internal rotation in functionally aligned robotic-assisted total knee arthroplasty does not impair functional outcomes.
articles.authors: Philpott A, Lambers A, Lee S, Collopy D, Clark G
articles.population: Patients undergoing functionally aligned robotic-assisted total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Forgotten Joint Score, knee flexion and extension range of motion, pain scores, and functional outcome measures at 1 year follow-up
articles.take_home: Femoral component internal rotation during robotic-assisted knee replacement did not worsen patient outcomes or satisfaction compared with standard positioning. This finding supports further investigation of alternative alignment strategies that may involve some internal rotation of the femoral component.

articles.title: Surgeon experience in hip arthroscopy improves operative efficiency and reduces conversion to total hip arthroplasty: A meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ramadanov N, Heinz M, Hable R, Becker R, Banke IJ
articles.population: Patients undergoing hip arthroscopy
articles.sample_size: 2624 procedures (1291 early experience cases, 1333 late experience cases) across 18 studies
articles.outcomes: Operative time, traction time, functional outcomes (Harris Hip Score, Non-Arthritic Hip Score), complications, revision arthroscopy, and conversion to total hip arthroplasty
articles.take_home: Surgeons with more experience in hip arthroscopy complete procedures faster and achieve better patient-reported functional improvements compared with less experienced surgeons. Conversion to hip replacement surgery was much lower in experienced surgeons' cases, though complication rates were similar between groups.

articles.title: The long-term outcome of the medial pivot knee arthroplasty system with minimum 25 years follow up.
articles.authors: Macheras GA, Galanakos SP, Papadakis SA, Kateros K, Milaras C, Tzefronis D
articles.population: Patients who received the ADVANCE Medial Pivot Knee System for total knee replacement
articles.outcomes: Revision rate, patient satisfaction, range of motion, clinical and radiographic stability
articles.take_home: After 25 years, this knee replacement design showed good long-term results with 89% of implants still functioning without revision. Patients reported high satisfaction and maintained good knee movement.

articles.title: Stacked-Cone Constructs for Extensive Tibial and/or Femoral Bone Loss in Complex Primary and Revision TKA: A Multicenter Analysis of 84 Cases.
articles.authors: Kumaran P, Telang SS, Culler M, Palmer RC, Longjohn DB, Oakes DA, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary or revision total knee replacement with extensive bone loss treated with stacked-cone constructs
articles.outcomes: Five-year implant survivorship free from all-cause reoperation (56.5%), all-cause revision (65.2%), and stacked-cone construct revision for aseptic loosening (91.7%)
articles.take_home: Stacked-cone constructs performed well in preventing the implant from loosening, but patients in this group needed further surgery fairly often because of other complications like infection and implant fracture. This reflects how complex these cases are.

articles.title: Temporal Trends in Revision Burden Ratio for Hip and Knee Arthroplasty: A Comparative Analysis of Large National Registries.
articles.authors: de Geofroy B, Argenson JN, Sculco PK, Gonzalez JF, Lustig S, Micicoi G
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip and knee arthroplasty recorded in national registries from the UK, Australia, the United States, and Sweden
articles.sample_size: Not specified - data from four national arthroplasty registries analysed over approximately one decade
articles.outcomes: Revision burden ratio for hip and knee arthroplasty, temporal trends in revision rates, causes of revision including aseptic loosening, infection and periprosthetic fractures
articles.take_home: Knee replacements now account for a higher proportion of revision surgeries compared to hip replacements, with this trend driven by more infections and fractures occurring after knee surgery and fewer loosening problems overall. The study suggests that infection prevention strategies for knee replacements need greater focus.

articles.title: Hip Arthroscopy is Associated with Increased Risk of Total Hip Arthroplasty Failure and Increased Resource Allocation in Middle-Aged Total Hip Arthroplasty Patients.
articles.authors: Thom ML, Benaroch LR, McClure JA, Carter B, Welk B, Lanting BA, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective population-based study using administrative data
articles.population: Ontario residents aged 40 to 65 years undergoing primary total hip replacement for osteoarthritis between 2002 and 2023
articles.sample_size: 85,814 primary hip replacements (5,642 with prior hip arthroscopy)
articles.outcomes: 5-year revision risk, 30-day emergency department visits, hospital readmissions, major surgical complications, hospital length of stay, 1-year costs, outpatient visits, mortality, and chronic opioid use
articles.take_home: Patients who had hip arthroscopy before hip replacement had a higher risk of needing revision surgery within 5 years (4.9% versus 3.3%), though short-term complications were similar. They also had higher healthcare costs and more outpatient visits after their hip replacement.

articles.title: Risk Stratification in Periprosthetic Hip Fracture Surgery: The Risk Analysis Index is Superior to the Five-Item Modified Frailty Index for Predicting 30-Day Complications.
articles.authors: Jowkar N, Ruffner M, Koltenyuk V, Sokolik ID, Horsley H, Rohl M, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national surgical database
articles.population: Patients aged 18 years and over undergoing revision hip replacement or fracture fixation for periprosthetic femoral fracture
articles.outcomes: 30-day major and minor complications, readmission, reoperation, discharge to non-home setting, wound complications, venous thromboembolism, and mortality
articles.take_home: The Risk Analysis Index was better than the Modified Frailty Index at predicting which patients would die or need to go to a care facility rather than home after surgery for hip fractures around artificial hip joints. This tool may help doctors and patients make better decisions before surgery.

articles.title: Quadriceps tendon autograft provides comparable stability and functional outcomes with lower donor-site morbidity than bone-patellar tendon-bone: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 randomized controlled trials with GRADE evidence.
articles.authors: Khalafallah MA, Hall OK, Elmenawy Z, Ibrahim KSM, Ghoneim OM, Raizah A, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Adults undergoing primary anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction
articles.outcomes: Anterior stability (KT-1000), patient-reported outcomes (IKDC, Lysholm, KOOS, Tegner), graft failure rates, donor-site morbidity, pivot-shift, postoperative stiffness
articles.take_home: Quadriceps tendon grafts work as well as hamstring or bone-patellar tendon-bone grafts for knee stability and patient function, but cause less damage at the donor site compared to bone-patellar tendon-bone. Quadriceps tendon is a good alternative for ACL reconstruction with lower risk of problems from the graft harvest site.

articles.title: Defining sagittal knee phenotypes via monopedal static anterior tibial translation. Part 1: Translating weight-bearing sagittal position into clinical risk profiles.
articles.authors: Kayaalp ME, Kahraman HC, Erden T, Lutter C, Scheffler S, Taser O, et al.
articles.population: Not specified
articles.outcomes: Classification of three distinct sagittal knee phenotypes based on static anterior tibial translation: osseous anteriorization (increased posterior tibial slope and ACL loss), acquired soft-tissue anteriorization (from chronic injury and meniscal deficiency), and inherent soft-tissue anteriorization (generalised hyperlaxity)
articles.take_home: The study proposes a new way to classify different types of knee instability based on weight-bearing position rather than manual laxity tests, identifying three distinct patterns that can combine to increase stress on the knee joint. This framework may help explain why some knee reconstructions fail in certain biomechanical environments.

articles.title: Laxity phenotyping in total knee arthroplasty: A current concept article.
articles.authors: Graichen H, Avram GM, von Eisenhart-Rothe R, Hirschmann MT
articles.study_type: Current concept article and classification system development
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Classification of nine distinct knee laxity phenotypes based on coronal gap characteristics in extension and flexion; treatment algorithm for gap balancing
articles.take_home: Knee laxity varies significantly between patients, and surgeons should tailor their approach to each person's specific pattern rather than using a one-size-fits-all method. Small gaps can be corrected with bone cuts alone, medium gaps need soft-tissue work and special inserts, and large gaps may need more constrained implants.

articles.title: Augmentation does not improve clinical and radiological outcomes after repair of Stener-like medial collateral ligament (MCL) lesions: Comparative cohort at mid-term follow-up.
articles.authors: Olivieri R, Munoz JT, Koch M, Rojas R, Franulic N, Pineda T, et al.
articles.population: Patients aged 18 years or older with distal medial collateral ligament avulsion injuries treated surgically
articles.sample_size: 29 patients (19 isolated repair, 10 repair with augmentation)
articles.outcomes: Radiographic valgus stability on stress radiographs, Lysholm scores, Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score subscales, complication rates, and failure rates
articles.take_home: Adding biological tissue augmentation to MCL repair did not improve stability, function or reduce complications compared to repair alone. Simple repair without augmentation appears to work just as well for most patients with this type of knee ligament injury.

articles.title: Proximal tibia vara and tibial overhang are associated with inferior outcomes after medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Karaman Y, Veizi E, Guven S, Naldoven OF, Cirdi YU, Firat A, et al.
articles.population: Patients with medial compartment knee osteoarthritis who underwent medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 212 patients (118 normal tibia group, 94 tibia vara group)
articles.outcomes: Oxford Knee Score, Knee Society Score, Forgotten Joint Score-12, postoperative alignment correction, tibial component positioning
articles.take_home: Patients with a curved shin bone (proximal tibia vara) had worse pain and function scores after unicompartmental knee replacement compared to those with normal shin anatomy. Additionally, when the replacement component extended beyond the edge of the bone, all patients had worse outcomes regardless of shin shape.

articles.title: Age- and time-specific management of traumatic anterior shoulder instability: The 2024 ESSKA-ESA Formal Consensus. Part 2: Treatment and return to sports.
articles.authors: Alentorn-Geli E, Brilakis E, Angelo AC, Boe B, Ruiz-Iban M, Dyrna F, et al.
articles.study_type: Formal consensus methodology establishing evidence-based recommendations
articles.population: Patients with traumatic anterior shoulder instability, stratified by age (adolescents, young adults, older adults) and instability type (first-time or recurrent)
articles.sample_size: 35 clinically relevant questions reviewed by steering group, rating group and reader group; 23 questions on treatment and return to sport reported
articles.outcomes: Treatment recommendations for surgical versus conservative management based on bone loss and soft tissue injury; return-to-sport criteria including pain-free movement, stability, strength and sport-specific readiness; typical return to sport between 4 and 6 months depending on procedure and sport
articles.take_home: European shoulder specialists recommend individualised treatment decisions based on the amount of bone loss and soft tissue damage, with bone surgery considered for significant bone loss and limited immobilisation needed. Most people can return to sport within 4 to 6 months once they have full pain-free movement, stable shoulders and adequate strength.

articles.title: Contraindications of femoral implants in primary total hip arthroplasty : legal implications and strategies for orthopaedic surgeons.
articles.authors: Manzo MA, Pentland V, Khoshbin A, Atrey A
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Manufacturer-listed contraindications identified for femoral implants and their legal implications under negligence and informed consent law
articles.take_home: Many manufacturers list conditions like obesity and poor bone quality as contraindications for hip implants, but these do not automatically prevent their use if clinically justified. Surgeons need to know their implant-specific contraindications, base decisions on evidence, and clearly discuss any increased risks with patients to reduce legal liability.

articles.title: Classification and Outcomes of 214 Periprosthetic Patellar Fractures in Primary Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Ducharme MT, Selemon NA, Bedard NA, Spangehl MJ, Springer BD, Hidden KA, et al.
articles.population: Patients who sustained periprosthetic patellar fractures around primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: 10-year survivorship free of revision and reoperation; revision and reoperation rates by fracture type; mortality rates
articles.take_home: Fractures of the kneecap around knee replacements are uncommon, with most doing well long-term. However, more complex fractures (Types II and III) had higher rates of needing further surgery compared to simpler fractures (Type I).

articles.title: Anterior cruciate ligament revision risk is associated with mismatch between hamstring graft size and body size in football players.
articles.authors: Sandon A, Eriksson K, Forssblad M, Barenius B
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national registry data
articles.population: Football players who had primary anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction with hamstring autograft
articles.sample_size: 9448 players (6127 men, 3321 women) followed between 2014 and 2023
articles.outcomes: Revision anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction surgery; revision occurred in 771 players (8.2%)
articles.take_home: Smaller grafts were associated with higher revision risk, but mainly in taller male players (176 cm or above). For tall men, smaller grafts had 2 to 3 times higher revision risk compared with larger grafts. This relationship was not found in women or shorter men, suggesting graft size should be matched to a player's height rather than using a one-size-fits-all approach.

articles.title: Vascular complications and reduction outcomes between direct versus transferred emergency room presentation of knee dislocations: a retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Wagoner M, Ponnada R, Chandler B, Israel H, Ali A
articles.population: Patients with radiographically confirmed knee dislocations
articles.sample_size: 41 patients (24 transferred from outside hospitals, 17 presented directly)
articles.outcomes: Vascular injury, neurological injury, and limb loss
articles.take_home: Knee dislocations that were not reduced before transfer from other hospitals had a much higher risk of blood vessel damage and amputation compared with those reduced promptly at the trauma centre, with delayed treatment being the key factor. Early recognition and quick reduction of the dislocation appear critical to preventing these serious complications.

articles.title: Efficacy of Intraosseous Tranexamic Acid in Primary Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Prospective Randomized Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Chen Y, Cao L, Ji B, Li G, Zhang X
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement
articles.outcomes: Haemoglobin reduction from day of surgery through three days after surgery, blood loss, transfusion rates, and adverse events
articles.take_home: Intraosseous tranexamic acid reduced blood loss as well as intravenous or topical administration in hip replacement surgery. The intraosseous approach showed lower calculated blood loss in the first two days, though the clinical importance of this is unclear and transfusion rates were similar across all groups.

articles.title: United States Trends in Utilization and One-Year Revision Risk of Cementless Total Knee Arthroplasty: An Analysis of American Joint Replacement Registry Data.
articles.authors: Hilow H, Tanenbaum J, Zaniletti I, Versteeg G, Manning D, Edelstein A
articles.study_type: Registry analysis of trends and outcomes
articles.population: Patients aged 65 years and older undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty in the United States
articles.sample_size: 1,178,783 primary TKAs from 2012 to 2024; 685,274 in CMS-linked cohort
articles.outcomes: One-year revision rates and reasons for revision comparing cementless versus cemented fixation
articles.take_home: Cementless knee replacements are being used increasingly in the US, and whilst they had slightly higher one-year revision rates than cemented versions, the actual difference was small and may not matter clinically. Cementless fixation showed higher risk for specific problems like loosening and instability.

articles.title: Operative Cultures Rarely Necessitate Change to Periprosthetic Joint Infection Antibiotic Plan Based on Monomicrobial Preoperative Aspiration.
articles.authors: Ducharme MT, Elmenawi KA, Poilvache H, Hannon CP, Abdel MP, Tande AJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients with periprosthetic joint infections treated at a single institution
articles.sample_size: 925 infected joints (613 knees, 312 hips) in 831 patients
articles.outcomes: Concordance between preoperative aspiration cultures and operative cultures; appropriateness of antibiotic treatment plans based on preoperative culture results
articles.take_home: In 70% of periprosthetic joint infections, preoperative aspiration identified a single organism, and antibiotic plans based on this result were appropriate in 97% of cases. Only 3% of infections required a change in antibiotics when operative cultures were reviewed, suggesting antibiotic treatment can usually be planned before surgery rather than waiting for intraoperative results.

articles.title: Preoperative fall history is associated with increased postoperative complications at 2 years after primary total knee arthroplasty: A propensity-matched analysis.
articles.authors: Kang H, Ilyas MH, Freeman IA, Sampson WT, Mannina CM, Kwon YM
articles.population: Patients aged 65 years and older undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: 90-day postoperative complications, falls, emergency department visits, readmissions, surgical site infections, medical complications, revision surgery, periprosthetic joint infection, periprosthetic fracture, and 2-year mortality
articles.take_home: Older patients who had fallen in the year before knee replacement surgery had significantly higher rates of complications after surgery, including more falls, infections and the need for further surgery, and were more likely to die within 2 years compared to those without a fall history. The study suggests doctors should assess fall risk before surgery and provide extra support during recovery.

articles.title: Biomechanical phenotypes of 90 degrees change of direction in football players: Unsupervised machine learning in anterior cruciate ligament injury prevention.
articles.authors: Della Villa F, Ghibellini A, Buckthorpe M, Bononi L, Gabbrielli M, Grassi A, et al.
articles.study_type: Cohort study using unsupervised machine learning analysis
articles.population: Healthy young football players
articles.outcomes: Four distinct biomechanical phenotypes identified during 90-degree change of direction movements, classified by force levels and movement control quality
articles.take_home: Researchers used artificial intelligence to identify four different movement patterns in young footballers during quick turns, ranging from poor control with low forces to good control with high forces. This could help identify which players need specific injury prevention training to reduce anterior cruciate ligament injury risk.

articles.title: A simple planning tool for tibial slope osteotomy-Osteotomy depth is a precise parameter to determine wedge height.
articles.authors: Vieider RP, Watrinet JM, Bilodeau R, Dadoo S, Vargas L, Mattar LT, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study of surgical planning methods
articles.population: Patients with posterior tibial slope of 13 degrees or more requiring knee osteotomy surgery
articles.outcomes: Accuracy of three wedge height planning methods compared to reference angle-based planning, measured by correction error within plus or minus 1 degree
articles.take_home: A planning method using osteotomy depth and correction angle was accurate in 83% of cases, whilst simple fixed ratios were accurate in only 50% or less. Using osteotomy depth helps surgeons plan tibial slope correction more precisely.

articles.title: Risk factors for manipulation under anesthesia after total knee arthroplasty and subsequent revision arthroplasty: a Finnish register-based study of 154,883 patients.
articles.authors: Sala J, Sirola J, Jaroma A, Kroger H, Sund R
articles.population: Patients aged 40 and over who had primary total knee replacement in Finland between 1999 and 2020
articles.outcomes: Risk of needing manipulation under anesthesia within 1 year of primary knee replacement, and risk of revision knee replacement within 10 years
articles.take_home: About 1 in 40 knee replacement patients needed manipulation under anesthesia within a year. Women, younger patients, and those with diabetes, heart disease or high cholesterol were at higher risk. Patients who had manipulation were twice as likely to need revision surgery, with a 15% risk over 10 years.

articles.title: Association between intraoperative fluoroscopy for anterior approach total hip arthroplasty and short-term revision risk: an analysis based on 49,878 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Vink MC, Zijlstra WP, Van Steenbergen LN, Van Veen PA, Doorn PF, Kuiper JWP, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement via direct anterior approach
articles.outcomes: Revision surgery rates at 6 months, 1 year, 2 years and 2.5 years
articles.take_home: Using fluoroscopy (live X-ray imaging) during hip replacement surgery was associated with a slightly lower risk of needing revision surgery, though revision rates were low in both groups and the clinical difference was small.

articles.title: Predicting persistent pain after total knee arthroplasty using different machine learning algorithms.
articles.authors: Rajamaki A, Reito A, Karsikas M, Niemelainen M, Eskelinen A
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using machine learning
articles.population: Patients who underwent primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Persistent pain, low Oxford Knee Score, and failure to achieve minimal clinically important difference in Oxford Knee Score at 1 year after surgery
articles.take_home: Despite using advanced machine learning and 751 patient variables, the researchers could not build a reliable model to predict which patients would have persistent pain or poor function after knee replacement surgery. This suggests that pain and functional outcomes after knee replacement are difficult to predict based on current available information.

articles.title: Association between spasticity of the hip and development of hip displacement in children: a cohort study of 786 hips.
articles.authors: Bakhtiyar M, Iljazi A, Odgaard A, Wong C, Petersen MM, Balslev-Clausen A
articles.population: Children with cerebral palsy classified as GMFCS levels IV-V (non-ambulant)
articles.outcomes: Hip displacement (migration percentage of 30% or more) and association with hip spasticity measured using Modified Ashworth Scale
articles.take_home: Children with cerebral palsy who have greater muscle tightness (spasticity) in their hips, particularly in the inner thigh muscles, are at higher risk of developing hip displacement. This association was stronger as spasticity levels increased.

articles.title: Registration and reporting characteristics of trials investigating exercise therapy following total knee arthroplasty: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Gronfeldt BM, Husted RS, Brodsgaard RH, Holst L, Kallemose T, Chapple CM, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty for osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Effect sizes of exercise therapy interventions; risk of bias; adherence to intention-to-treat analysis; dropout and adverse event reporting
articles.take_home: Trials that registered their main outcome before starting showed smaller, more modest benefits from exercise therapy after knee replacement surgery, along with better reporting of dropouts and side effects. Most trials did not clearly specify what their main outcome would be beforehand, which can make results less trustworthy.

articles.title: Mid- to long-term outcomes of short stem total hip arthroplasty in patients younger than 55 years: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Elbanna M, Vignaraja V, Kyriakides J, Tsamados S, Lewis TL, Bansal M, et al.
articles.population: Patients younger than 55 years undergoing short-stem total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Harris Hip Score improvement (45.3 to 93.8), complication rates (intraoperative fracture 1.6%, dislocation 0.6%, infection 0.4%), stem survivorship free from aseptic loosening 99.7%, overall revision rate 1.6%
articles.take_home: Short-stem hip replacements in patients under 55 years show excellent results with high implant survival rates of 99.7% and low complication rates, though the authors note that more high-quality research is needed to confirm these findings.

articles.title: Clinical and Radiographic Outcomes at a Mean of 7 Years Following Primary Transfibular Total Ankle Arthroplasty in Younger and Older Patients.
articles.authors: Schafer KA, Day J, Motsay M, Zhang H, Zhang Z, Schon LC
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary transfibular total ankle arthroplasty, divided into younger (<55 years) and older (≥55 years) groups
articles.sample_size: 251 ankles in 236 patients (72 ankles in younger group, 179 in older group)
articles.outcomes: Implant survivorship, patient-reported outcome measures (SF-12, Ankle Osteoarthritis Scale, pain scale), radiographic alignment, range of motion, periprosthetic cysts, adverse events, and reoperations at mean 7 years follow-up
articles.take_home: Transfibular total ankle replacement showed good midterm results in both younger and older patients, with similar complication rates and functional improvements regardless of age. This suggests the procedure may be a suitable treatment option for end-stage ankle arthritis across a wide age range.
